IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an important subproblem in Japanese predicate-argument structure analysis and presents empirically stronger models (Grid-RNN multi-sequence and deep residual stacked Bi-RNNs) that improve SOTA on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5, notably for zero-argument identification. These results are likely to influence practitioners working on intra-sentential PAS and methods that avoid syntactic features, and the finding that residual connections enable deeper RNN/Grid models is practically useful. However, impact is tempered by the narrow evaluation (single corpus, intra-sentential scope only) and limited evidence that the approach generalizes beyond this subfield or opens substantial new theoretical directions, so I judge the contribution to be of moderate importance. | B(3): The paper tackles an important applied problem (Japanese predicate-argument structure analysis) and demonstrates clear empirical gains over prior strong baselines on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5, notably improving zero-argument identification without relying on external syntactic features. These results are practically useful and suggest a viable neural alternative to feature-heavy methods, but the scope is limited to intra-sentential arguments and a single language/corpus, and code release is not mentioned which lowers immediate reproducibility and downstream uptake. Overall the contribution is a solid, useful advance that is likely to be built upon within this subfield but is not transformative across NLP more broadly.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important subproblem in Japanese predicate-argument structure analysis and presents empirically stronger models (Grid-RNN multi-sequence and deep residual stacked Bi-RNNs) that improve SOTA on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5, notably for zero-argument identification. These results are likely to influence practitioners working on intra-sentential PAS and methods that avoid syntactic features, and the finding that residual connections enable deeper RNN/Grid models is practically useful. However, impact is tempered by the narrow evaluation (single corpus, intra-sentential scope only) and limited evidence that the approach generalizes beyond this subfield or opens substantial new theoretical directions, so I judge the contribution to be of moderate importance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important applied problem (Japanese predicate-argument structure analysis) and demonstrates clear empirical gains over prior strong baselines on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5, notably improving zero-argument identification without relying on external syntactic features. These results are practically useful and suggest a viable neural alternative to feature-heavy methods, but the scope is limited to intra-sentential arguments and a single language/corpus, and code release is not mentioned which lowers immediate reproducibility and downstream uptake. Overall the contribution is a solid, useful advance that is likely to be built upon within this subfield but is not transformative across NLP more broadly.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (Grid-RNN multi-sequence model, residual/depth benefits, improved zero-argument identification). Notation and methodological description appear consistent (stacked Bi-RNNs, Grid-RNNs with GRUs and residuals) and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility. Some presentation elements — e.g. the effectiveness and placement of figures/tables and more explicit isolation of individual ablations — could be clearer to better connect architectural choices to the reported gains, but the writing quality is otherwise good and unambiguous. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: Grid-RNN multi-sequence modeling without syntactic features and improved zero-argument identification. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples (e.g., comparisons to Imamura et al. and Ouchi et al.) effectively support claims. Writing quality is generally good with adequate related-work coverage, though a few minor presentation choices (e.g., explicit callouts about code availability and clearer labeling of some architectural diagrams) could improve reproducibility and reader clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (Grid-RNN multi-sequence model, residual/depth benefits, improved zero-argument identification). Notation and methodological description appear consistent (stacked Bi-RNNs, Grid-RNNs with GRUs and residuals) and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility. Some presentation elements — e.g. the effectiveness and placement of figures/tables and more explicit isolation of individual ablations — could be clearer to better connect architectural choices to the reported gains, but the writing quality is otherwise good and unambiguous.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: Grid-RNN multi-sequence modeling without syntactic features and improved zero-argument identification. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples (e.g., comparisons to Imamura et al. and Ouchi et al.) effectively support claims. Writing quality is generally good with adequate related-work coverage, though a few minor presentation choices (e.g., explicit callouts about code availability and clearer labeling of some architectural diagrams) could improve reproducibility and reader clarity.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claims are clear, baselines and ablations are sensible, and the model is well‑specified, so the methodology is largely sound. Deductions reflect consistent concerns about missing statistical significance testing, limited reproducibility (code release), and lack of targeted qualitative analysis to more directly link gains to linguistic phenomena; these are important but do not undermine the overall technical validity, so a score of 4 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and provides experiments on NAIST 1.5 showing improvements over reasonable baselines (Imamura et al., Ouchi et al.), and reports hyperparameters and that deeper networks with residuals help. However, the summary does not indicate statistical significance testing for main comparisons nor whether ablations cleanly isolate each architectural choice (Grid vs. Bi‑RNN, residuals, depth), which I expect given the claims about why the architecture captures multi‑predicate interactions. There is also no mention of targeted qualitative error analysis connecting improvements to specific linguistic phenomena (e.g., zero vs. Dep distinctions). Taken together, the methodology is promising but missing key controls and statistical rigor required to fully support the main technical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and provides experiments on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 showing improvements over reimplemented and joint-model baselines, with sensible ablations (depth, residuals) that support the technical claims. There are no mathematical proofs required beyond model definitions, which are standard and appear complete, but the summary does not mention released code or statistical significance testing, limiting reproducibility and certainty about gains. Overall the methodology is sound with appropriate baselines and hyperparameter reporting, so only minor issues (reproducibility/statistics) prevent a top score.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work is an incremental but useful application of existing techniques (Grid-RNNs, Bi-RNNs, residual connections) to model multi-predicate interactions for Japanese PAS, offering empirical gains without external syntax. It is not a major conceptual breakthrough nor does it provide a formal theoretical account, so a moderate originality rating reflects the paper's practical novelty but limited methodological novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a moderately novel application by adapting Grid-RNNs and deep stacked Bi-GRUs with residual connections to model multi-predicate interactions for Japanese PAS, distinguishing itself from prior pointwise and ALLCases models by learning interactions from sequences rather than syntax. However, the core components (Grid-RNN, Bi-RNNs, residuals, depth) are existing techniques and the work reads as an empirical architecture adaptation rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm; it also lacks a formal theoretical account explaining why these choices capture predicate-argument structure. Thus the contribution is an incremental but useful methodological advance rather than a major conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a notable architectural novelty—applying a Grid-RNN multi-sequence model (with residual connections and deep stacked Bi-RNNs) to capture multi-predicate interactions directly from word sequences, avoiding syntactic features. Predicate argument structure analysis is a well-studied task, so the problem formulation itself is not new, but the method clearly differentiates from prior joint models (e.g., ALLCases) and offers a non-trivial extension by modeling interactions across predicates end-to-end. An expert would find the approach informative because it demonstrates that deeper Grid-style sequence models can yield practical gains (especially on zero-argument identification) without external syntax, though the contribution is incremental rather than a radical departure.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong fit for a top NLP venue: it addresses a core PAS task, uses appropriate modern neural architectures (Grid‑RNN/stacked Bi‑RNNs), and provides sound empirical evaluation against relevant baselines. While Reviewer A notes a minor scope limitation (no inter‑sentential arguments) and could ask for clearer theoretical motivation for the Grid design, these do not undermine the paper’s overall appropriateness for the intended audience, so a top score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem—Japanese predicate argument structure analysis—using neural sequence/Grid-RNN models and evaluation on the standard NAIST Text Corpus, which aligns well with top-tier NLP/ML conference scope and audience. Methods (stacked Bi‑RNNs/Grid‑RNNs with residual connections), baselines (Imamura; Ouchi et al.), and F‑measure evaluation meet community expectations. I note a minor scope limitation: the focus on intra‑sentential (Dep/Zero) cases and omission of inter‑sentential arguments reduces broader applicability, and the work would benefit from clearer theoretical motivation for the Grid architecture; otherwise it is a strong fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it tackles a core semantic analysis task (Japanese PAS) with modern neural architectures (Grid-RNN, stacked Bi-RNNs with residuals), demonstrates clear empirical gains over prior SOTA on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5—notably on the challenging zero-argument case—and reports hyperparameters. The methodology and evaluation (comparisons to Imamura et al. and Ouchi et al., F-measure) meet community standards, and the work is positioned squarely for the NLP audience interested in structured semantic prediction and practical model improvements.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares to appropriate, state-of-the-art baselines on the standard NAIST corpus, reports metrics and hyperparameters, and provides useful analyses of modeling choices. However, Reviewer A’s point about missing statistical significance testing, a more detailed error analysis by linguistic category, and clearer isolated ablations are valid shortcomings that prevent full confidence in the claimed gains, so I rate it a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Imamura et al. reimplementation and Ouchi et al. ALLCases) on the standard NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 using F-measure, so the experimental setup and metrics appear fair. However, the summary does not report statistical significance testing or a detailed qualitative error analysis linking gains to linguistic categories (e.g., zero vs. Dep vs. Inter-Zero), nor is it clear that each modelling choice (Grid vs. Bi-RNN, residuals, depth) is fully isolated in ablations. Related work coverage is adequate and prior methods are compared, but the lack of significance tests and deeper error analyses are important omissions given the theoretical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against the appropriate state-of-the-art baselines (Imamura et al. reimplementation and Ouchi et al. 2015 ALLCases) on the same NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 using F-measure, and reports hyperparameters so results appear reproducible. Differences are analyzed (single- vs multi-sequence Grid-RNN, depth/residual effects) and the manuscript explicitly states limitations (no inter-sentential arguments), indicating fair and transparent comparison to prior work. Related work coverage is noted as adequate, and the claims of outperforming prior methods are supported by the provided evaluations.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental work is solid and shows clear gains on the NAIST corpus, with appropriate baselines, reported hyperparameters, and acknowledgement of the inter-sentential limitation. I deduct one point because evaluation is confined to a single dataset and code release is not mentioned, and some additional analyses (e.g., more explicit statistical significance testing and deeper error categorization) would strengthen confidence and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are adequate in that the Grid-RNN and deep residual Bi-RNNs are evaluated on NAIST and compared to strong baselines with reported hyperparameters and a stated limitation regarding inter-sentential arguments. However, the summary indicates missing elements I require: no mention of statistical significance testing for main F-measure gains, limited qualitative/error analysis linking errors to linguistic categories (e.g., zero-argument vs syntactic confusions), and insufficient evidence that each modeling choice (Grid vs single-sequence, residuals, depth) was isolated via ablation. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility would be strengthened by released code.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are strong: the proposed Grid-RNN and single-sequence Bi-RNN models are evaluated against reasonable baselines on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with reported F-measures and show clear gains, especially on zero-argument identification. The paper includes useful sensitivity/ablation-style analyses (effect of residual connections and increased depth) and reports hyperparameters, and it explicitly acknowledges the important limitation of not handling inter-sentential arguments. I deduct one point because evaluation is limited to a single corpus and code release is not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility and the claims' generality.

main_claims: Introduces a Grid-RNN-based multi-sequence neural model that captures multi-predicate interactions from word sequence information without relying on syntactic features.
The proposed neural sequence models (single-sequence and multi-sequence) outperform prior state-of-the-art baselines on NAIST Text Corpus 1.5, particularly improving zero-argument identification.
Residual connections and increased network depth (e.g., 8 layers) improve performance, enabling effective learning of deeper RNN/Grid models.
_summary.methodology: Use stacked Bi-RNNs (single-sequence) and Grid-RNNs (multi-sequence) with GRU units and residual connections to produce hidden representations per word/predicate, and classify case labels (NOM, ACC, DAT, PRED, null) via a softmax trained with cross-entropy.
baselines: Imamura et al. (2009) pointwise model (reimplemented by Ouchi et al.)
Ouchi et al. (2015) ALLCases joint model
limitations: The work focuses only on intra-sentential arguments (Dep and Zero) and does not address inter-sentential (Inter-Zero) argument identification, which requires searching broader document context and is harder.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Japanese Predicate Argument Structure Analysis

reviewer_a.name: Formalist Theorist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a research focus on formal models of predicate-argument structure and interpretable linguistic representations. Extensive experience reviewing theoretical and formal work for ACL and TACL.
traits: Penalises papers that present primarily empirical/engineering changes without clear formal or linguistic motivation (deducts points when novel architectures lack a theoretical account of why they capture the targeted phenomena).
Penalises papers that do not include statistical significance testing for main comparisons (expects p-values or confidence intervals for key SOTA claims).

Penalises papers lacking detailed qualitative error analysis that connects model behavior to linguistic categories (expects focused analysis for zero-argument cases and syntactic vs. semantic error patterns).

Requires clear ablations isolating each modeling choice (e.g., residual connections, depth, Grid vs. Bi-RNN) and will drop scores if core claims rely on combined changes without isolating their contributions.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I judge novelty by theoretical/linguistic insight and demand statistical rigor and targeted analyses that explain why an architecture succeeds on the linguistic phenomena of interest.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in applied NLP with a track record of deploying sequence models in production and reviewing empirical NLP work for EMNLP/CoNLL. Focuses on model design, reproducible experiments, and meaningful empirical gains on established benchmarks.
traits: Rewards papers that achieve clear empirical improvements on standard benchmarks and practical sub-tasks (gives credit for SOTA gains, especially on challenging phenomena like zero-argument identification).

Rewards comprehensive empirical evaluation and ablations that demonstrate robustness (expects multiple model sizes/depths and training recipes and will up-score papers that explore these systematically).

Prefers papers that avoid heavy feature/syntax engineering in favor of end-to-end neural models (gives bonus for approaches that remove dependency on external syntactic features and still improve performance).

Values reproducibility and will score up if authors provide training details, hyperparameters, and commit to releasing code/datasets; will penalise lightly if these are missing but otherwise impressed by solid empirical methodology.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize tangible empirical contributions and practicality: did the paper advance performance or robustness on an important task with clear, reproducible experiments?
